module_category: General Skills: Orientation and Analyzing Behavior
title: Biosocial Theory

pages.text:
14
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
General Handout 5 (p. 1 of 2)
Biosocial Theory
Why do I have so much trouble controlling
my emotions and my actions?
Emotional vulnerability is biological:
It’s simply how some people are born.

 They are more sensitive to emotional stimuli; they can detect subtle
emotional information in the environment that others don’t even notice.

 They experience emotions much more often than others.

 Their emotions seem to hit for no reason, from out of the blue.

 They have more intense emotions.

 Their emotions hit like a ton of bricks.

 And their emotions are long-lasting.
Impulsivity also has a biological basis:
Regulating action is harder for some than for others.

 They find it very hard to restrain impulsive behaviors.

 Often, without thinking, they do things that get them in trouble.

 Sometimes their behavior seems to come out of nowhere.

 They find it very hard to be effective.

 Their moods get in the way of organizing to achieve their goals.

 They cannot control behaviors linked to their moods.
(continued on next page)

15
General Handout 5 (p. 2 of 2)
An invalidating social environment
can make it very hard to regulate emotions.

 An invalidating environment doesn’t seem to understand your emotions.

 It tells you your emotions are invalid, weird, wrong, or bad.

 It often ignores your emotional reactions and does nothing to help you.

 It may say things like “Don’t be such a baby!” “Quit your blubbering.” “Quit
being such a chicken and just solve the problem.” or “Normal people don’t
get this frustrated.”

 People who invalidate are often doing the best they can.

 They may not know how to validate or how important it is to validate, or
they may be afraid that if they validate your emotions, you will get more
emotional, not less.

 They may be under high stress or time pressure, or they may have too
few resources themselves.

 There may be just a poor fit between you and your social environment:
You may be a tulip in a rose garden.
An ineffective social environment
is a big problem when you want
to learn to regulate emotions and actions.

 Your environment may reinforce out-of-control emotions and actions.

 If people give in when you get out of control, it will be hard for you to get in
control.

 If others command you to change, but don’t coach you on how to do this,
it will be hard to keep on trying to change.
It’s the transactions that count
between the person and the social environment.

 Biology and the social environment influence the person.

 The person reciprocates and influences his or her social environment.

 The social environment reciprocates and influences the person.

 And so on and on and on.

General Handout 5 (p. 1 of 2)
Biosocial Theory
Why do I have so much trouble controlling
my emotions and my actions?
Emotional vulnerability is biological:
It’s simply how some people are born.

 They are more sensitive to emotional stimuli; they can detect subtle
emotional information in the environment that others don’t even notice.





 They have more intense emotions.

 Their emotions hit like a ton of bricks.

 And their emotions are long-lasting.
Impulsivity also has a biological basis:
Regulating action is harder for some than for others.

 They find it very hard to restrain impulsive behaviors.



 Sometimes their behavior seems to come out of nowhere.

 They find it very hard to be effective.

 Their moods get in the way of organizing to achieve their goals.

 They cannot control behaviors linked to their moods.
(continued on next page) General Handout 5 (p. 2 of 2)
An invalidating social environment
can make it very hard to regulate emotions.

 An invalidating environment doesn’t seem to understand your emotions.

 It tells you your emotions are invalid, weird, wrong, or bad.

 It often ignores your emotional reactions and does nothing to help you.


being such a chicken and just solve the problem.” or “Normal people don’t



they may be afraid that if they validate your emotions, you will get more




 There may be just a poor fit between you and your social environment:
You may be a tulip in a rose garden.
An ineffective social environment
is a big problem when you want
to learn to regulate emotions and actions.

 Your environment may reinforce out-of-control emotions and actions.


control.


it will be hard to keep on trying to change.
It’s the transactions that count
between the person and the social environment.

 Biology and the social environment influence the person.

 The person reciprocates and influences his or her social environment.

 The social environment reciprocates and influences the person.

 And so on and on and on.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Biosocial Theory
ourspace_kindness_pass.category: DBT General Orientation and Analyzing Behavior

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.routine_role: monthly review or larger pattern tool
routine_integration.start_the_day_use: not auto-loaded on survival days; use as a scheduled monthly review item
routine_integration.recommended_block: monthly support audit or household reset
